title: Feature Map Depth vs. Spatial Size Tradeoff
concept: As a CNN goes deeper, feature maps typically shrink spatially (via stride or pooling) while growing in channel depth (more filters), trading precise positional detail for a richer set of learned pattern types — the funnel shape behind every classic CNN architecture.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do deep-layer filters in a trained CNN typically end up detecting, compared to early-layer filters?

options.text: More complex things, like object parts or whole objects, built from the simple patterns earlier layers found
The exact same simple edges as the very first layer
Nothing — deep filters don't detect anything meaningful

exercises.explain: Right — complexity grows with depth. Now: what happens to the SIZE and COUNT of those feature maps as you go deeper?

cards.title: Zooming out while adding experts
cards.body: Picture a photo editing pipeline where every step shrinks the picture a little... but also hands it to twice as many specialists, each looking for something different. You're trading a big, simple view for a small, richly-analyzed one.

cards.title: Smaller and smaller, but deeper and deeper
cards.body: Going deeper into a CNN, feature maps typically get spatially smaller — fewer rows and columns — because of stride or pooling. At the very same time, they typically get channel-wise deeper — more feature maps stacked together — because later layers usually use more filters. You trade fine positional detail for a richer set of pattern types.

cards.title: A shrinking map with more layers of detail
cards.body: Think of a road atlas: a page showing a whole country is spatially huge but has almost no detail. A page zoomed into one city block is spatially tiny — but you can add a dozen extra detail layers on it: traffic, terrain, buildings, zoning. CNNs make that same trade as they go deeper: less spatial area, more layers of 'what kind of pattern is here.'

cards.title: Watching the numbers shrink and grow
cards.body: A toy CNN might process a 64x64 image with 3 channels like this: layer 1 outputs 64x64 with 16 feature maps. Layer 2 uses a stride that halves the size: 32x32 with 32 feature maps. Layer 3: 16x16 with 64 feature maps. Layer 4: 8x8 with 128 feature maps. Notice the spatial size keeps halving while the channel count keeps doubling.

cards.title: Shrinking isn't losing — it's trading
cards.body: It's easy to assume that shrinking a feature map means throwing information away for no reason. It's really a deliberate trade: you give up precise WHERE information in exchange for a much richer set of WHAT-kind-of-pattern information. That funnel shape — wide and shallow at the start, narrow and deep at the end — is the exact blueprint every classic CNN architecture is built on.

cards.title: Shallow Layer vs. Deep Layer
cards.body: The size/depth tradeoff in one glance:

exercises.prompt: As you go deeper into a typical CNN, what usually happens to feature maps?

options.text: They get spatially smaller but channel-wise deeper (more feature maps)
They get spatially bigger but channel-wise shallower
Both spatial size and channel count shrink together

exercises.explain: Right — smaller spatially, deeper channel-wise. That's the classic CNN funnel shape.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A feature map shrinking in spatial size as you go deeper means the network is losing useful information for no reason.
exercises.explain: False — it's a deliberate tradeoff: less precise spatial detail in exchange for many more learned pattern types (channels).

exercises.sentence: Going deeper into a CNN typically trades spatial ___ for channel-wise depth.
exercises.explain: Size — spatial size shrinks while channel depth grows, as you move deeper into the network.

exercises.prompt: Which best describes the overall shape most classic CNN architectures follow, from input to output?

options.text: Wide and spatially large but channel-shallow at the start, narrowing spatially while growing channel-deep toward the end
Constant spatial size and constant channel count all the way through
Spatially growing bigger and bigger while channels shrink to 1

exercises.explain: Exactly — that funnel shape is the backbone behind classic architectures like LeNet, AlexNet, and VGG.

exercises.prompt: A CNN's feature maps go from 128x128 with 8 channels (layer 1) down to 8x8 with 256 channels (layer 6). What tradeoff does this pattern demonstrate, and is it a sign of a healthy design?

options.text: It trades spatial detail for channel-wise richness as depth increases — this shrinking-but-deepening funnel is exactly the pattern classic CNN architectures are built on, so yes, it's healthy
It's a bug, because feature maps should always stay the same size and channel count at every layer
It shows the network is failing, since spatial size should always grow, never shrink
It shows the network only cares about channels and has abandoned spatial information completely

exercises.explain: Exactly right! That shrink-spatially / grow-channels funnel is deliberate and is the exact backbone every classic CNN — LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, and beyond — is built on. That wraps up Feature Maps! Next up: 1x1 convolutions — the strange-looking filter that's all about reshaping channels.
